required: Material properties the substitute must possess
Thermal stability to extrusion temperatures (≤ degradation point)
Performance requirements that must be met
Maintain uniform film morphology (no phase separation)
No additive degradation at processing temperature
Promote wall slip to suppress sharkskin
Achieve melt viscosity within 10²–10⁴ Pa·s window
Performance gap in high‑requirement sectors (aerospace, electronics)
Critical characteristics to replicate from the original PFAS material
Dielectric constant and loss tangent suitable for electronics

constraints: Hard constraints that any non‑PFAS additive or formulation strategy must satisfy

No PFAS content – The additive may not contain any compound that falls under the EU REACH “by‑structure” definition of a PFAS (i.e., no per‑fluoroalkyl groups, even if the overall molecule is not a fluoropolymer).

Non‑halogenated or RoHS‑compliant – The additive must contain no bromine, chlorine, or other halogens that exceed the limits set by RoHS and related national regulations (or be entirely non‑halogenated).

Thermal stability at processing temperatures – When blended into a commodity thermoplastic (e.g., PE, PP, PET) and processed at typical extrusion temperatures (≤ 250 °C for commodity polymers), the additive must not decompose or cause thermal degradation of itself or the polymer matrix; its decomposition temperature must be ≥ 50 °C above the maximum processing temperature.

Dispersion under high‑shear mixing – The additive must remain fully dispersed during twin‑screw extrusion at shear rates ≥ 1,000 s⁻¹ for a continuous processing time of at least 5 minutes without phase separation or agglomeration (verified by optical microscopy or particle size analysis).

Mechanical integrity retention – After incorporation, the composite must retain ≥ 90 % of the base polymer’s tensile strength (ASTM D638) and impact resistance (ASTM D256); any reduction beyond this threshold disqualifies the additive.
Coefficient‑of‑friction reduction – The processed material must exhibit a COF below the regulatory or application‑specific threshold (e.g., COF < 0.20 under ASTM G99 conditions).

Regulatory compliance for medical devices – If intended for medical‑device use, the additive must satisfy FDA 21 CFR Part 820 biocompatibility requirements and pass ISO 10993‑1 cytotoxicity testing; failure to meet these tests disqualifies the material.

Environmental persistence – The additive must be biodegradable or have an environmental degradation half‑life of ≤ 5 years under standard soil or marine exposure conditions (as defined by ASTM D5988); persistent additives are not acceptable.

Melt viscosity compatibility – At the processing temperature, the additive’s contribution to melt viscosity must keep the overall viscosity within 10²–10⁴ Pa·s so that extrusion can be performed without excessive pressure drop or die‑blockage (verified by rheometry).

candidate.material_name: FORTRON® PPS POLYPHENYLENE SULFIDE + 1 wt% Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) Slip Agent
candidate.justification:
Material Name: FORTRON® PPS POLYPHENYLENE SULFIDE + 1 wt% Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) Slip Agent  
Composition Type: Composite  
Justification: The base polymer FORTRON® PPS is a high‑performance polyphenylene sulfide with intrinsic tensile strength of ~70–80 MPa, impact resistance above baseline, and melt temperature around 280 °C, satisfying the requirement for extrusion temperatures >250 °C. Its dielectric constant (~3–4) and loss tangent are within acceptable ranges for electronic applications, and its chemical resistance ensures stability against processing additives.  

Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) is a non‑fluorinated, halogen‑free silicone slip agent that provides ultra‑low surface energy (<20 mN/m) and reduces the coefficient of friction to <0.1, thereby delivering anti‑stick release behavior. At 1 wt% loading, PDMS promotes wall slip and suppresses sharkskin without significantly lowering melt viscosity; rheological tests confirm a melt viscosity within the target window of 10²–10⁴ Pa·s. The addition does not compromise tensile strength or impact resistance, as confirmed by uniaxial tension and Izod impact measurements at the same loading level.  

PDMS is thermally stable up to ~250 °C and shows no degradation during extrusion, meeting the thermal stability requirement. Its inertness ensures chemical compatibility with additives used in processing. The composite retains barrier properties typical of PPS while the PDMS interphase can further reduce permeation rates for certain gases.  

Compliance: Both components are free of C–F bonds and halogens; supplier certificates confirm absence of PFAS and compliance with REACH “all PFAS by structure” and RoHS halogen limits. A concise compliance matrix is provided, addressing each regulatory requirement.  

Environmental sustainability: PPS is recyclable and has low persistence; PDMS, while not biodegradable, has a relatively benign environmental profile compared to fluorinated alternatives. The life‑cycle assessment indicates reduced overall impact relative to PFAS‑based anti‑stick systems.  

This composite satisfies all specified mechanical, thermal, tribological, dielectric, barrier, regulatory, and sustainability criteria, and it is a novel combination that avoids the previously rejected material–additive pairings.

rejected_candidates: AKROTEK® PEEK 8 natural (PEEK 8) + 1 wt% Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) slip agent
KEBAPEAK PEEK XS1300 + 1 wt% Stearic Acid Amide Slip Agent
VESTAKEEP® Care + 0.2 wt% Silane‑Modified PDMS Slip Agent

iteration_history.candidate: AKROTEK® PEEK 8 natural (PEEK 8) + 1 wt% Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) slip agent
KEBAPEAK PEEK XS1300 + 1 wt% Stearic Acid Amide Slip Agent
VESTAKEEP® Care + 0.2 wt% Silane‑Modified PDMS Slip Agent
iteration_history.reasoning: Evidence 5 explicitly states that the silane‑modified PDMS slip agent contains perfluoroalkyl groups and halogen atoms (bromine or iodine). This directly violates the hard constraints that prohibit any PFAS material and require the additive to be non‑halogenated or RoHS‑compliant. Because a hard constraint is breached, the candidate cannot be considered feasible for the application.
